claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to ask you some questions about your child.

**First question: How old is your child?**

This determines which eligibility rules apply:
- If your child is **under 16**, there's an automatic qualification path
- If your child is **16 to 19**, we'll need to check their education or training status
- If your child is **20 or over**, they generally don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age (or which age category they fall into).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I'm trying to find out if I can claim Child Benefit for my son, Alex. He's 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me help you determine if you can claim Child Benefit for Alex, who is 8 years old.

Since Alex is under 16, let's continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Since Alex is 8 years old, he's under 16. Now I need to check a few more things.

**Next question: Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

For an 8-year-old, this would typically be a straightforward "yes" since children this age are generally in school and not in paid employment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh yes, he's not working at all - he's just in primary school like any other 8-year-old!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's what I expected. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now the next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in his own right?** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)

In other words, Alex doesn't claim benefits like ESA or Universal Credit himself, does he? For an 8-year-old in primary school, this would typically be "yes" (meaning he's NOT receiving these benefits).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name - he's just a child, so no ESA or Universal Credit or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now the next check:

**Is Alex NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?**

Since Alex is only 8 years old and in primary school, he's obviously not in any kind of apprenticeship. This would be "not in apprenticeship."

Can you confirm he's not in any apprenticeship program?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, he's definitely not in any apprenticeship - he's only 8 and still in primary school!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now we need to check your residency status. This is an important requirement for Child Benefit.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify through any of these routes:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (most common)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You're a Crown servant living abroad

**Which of these applies to your situation?** The most straightforward is if you normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm here in England with Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's the most straightforward route. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now the next important question:

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You can qualify through any of these routes:

1. **You live with Alex** (most common)
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit would be - for things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Based on what you've told me (you live in England with Alex), it sounds like you live with him. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Another family member

The situations are:
1. **No one else is claiming** (most straightforward)
2. Someone else is claiming but you've both agreed they should transfer the claim to you
3. There's a dispute about who should claim

Which situation applies to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check Alex's living situation:

**Where is Alex currently living?**

- Is he **in hospital**?
- Is he **in care** (local authority care)?
- Is he **fostered**?
- Or is he **living normally at home with you**?

Based on what you've told me, it sounds like he's living at home with you normally. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct - he's living at home with me, just a normal living situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Just one final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, the local council isn't contributing money towards Alex's living costs or upkeep, correct? 

Since Alex lives at home with you in a normal family situation, I expect the answer is "yes" (the council is NOT paying).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - the council isn't paying anything towards Alex. It's just me looking after him at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful news! Let me get the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
# 🎉 Great News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

**Result: ELIGIBLE**

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for Alex!

## Summary:
✅ Alex is 8 years old (under 16)  
✅ He's not in paid work  
✅ He's not receiving benefits in his own right  
✅ He's not in an apprenticeship  
✅ You live in the UK  
✅ You live with and are responsible for Alex  
✅ No one else is claiming for him  
✅ He lives at home with you  
✅ The council isn't paying towards his upkeep  

## Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware**: If you or your partner earn over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge (but you can still claim)
5. **Payments**: You'll receive payments every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

## How to Apply:
Visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Congratulations! This is a straightforward case, and you should be able to get your claim processed smoothly. Do you have any questions about the next steps?

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 8 years old, which is under 16 - meeting the age requirement; (2) He is not in paid work (he's in primary school); (3) He does not receive benefits like ESA or Universal Credit in his own right; (4) He is not in any apprenticeship program; (5) The claimant normally lives in the UK, meeting residency requirements; (6) The claimant lives with Alex and is responsible for him; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex lives at home in a normal living situation; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria have been met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all requirements to claim Child Benefit for their 8-year-old son Alex. Alex is under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK, lives with and is responsible for Alex, no one else is claiming for him, and the council is not contributing to his upkeep. This is a straightforward eligible case.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 8 years old, which is under 16 - meeting the age requirement; (2) He is not in paid work (he's in primary school); (3) He does not receive benefits like ESA or Universal Credit in his own right; (4) He is not in any apprenticeship program; (5) The claimant normally lives in the UK, meeting residency requirements; (6) The claimant lives with Alex and is responsible for him; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex lives at home in a normal living situation; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria have been met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all requirements to claim Child Benefit for their 8-year-old son Alex. Alex is under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK, lives with and is responsible for Alex, no one else is claiming for him, and the council is not contributing to his upkeep. This is a straightforward eligible case.
